questions.q: The Chandrasekhar Limit is the maximum mass that which kind of star can have before it collapses?
questions.explanation: The Chandrasekhar limit is the maximum mass a white dwarf can have while remaining stable against gravitational collapse.

questions.q: Approximately how many times the mass of the Sun is the Chandrasekhar Limit?
questions.explanation: The Chandrasekhar limit is roughly 1.4 solar masses.

questions.q: After whom is the Chandrasekhar Limit named?
questions.explanation: The limit is named after the Indian-American astrophysicist Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar.

questions.q: Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar was born in which country?
questions.explanation: Chandrasekhar was born in 1910 in Lahore, then in British India.

questions.q: Which major prize did Chandrasekhar win in 1983?
questions.explanation: Chandrasekhar shared the 1983 Nobel Prize in Physics for his studies of the structure and evolution of stars.

questions.q: A white dwarf is held up against gravity mainly by what?
questions.explanation: White dwarfs are supported by electron degeneracy pressure, a quantum-mechanical effect, not by ongoing fusion.

questions.q: If a white dwarf's mass exceeds the Chandrasekhar Limit, what is the likely outcome?
options: It collapses into a neutron star or black hole
It becomes a main-sequence star again
questions.explanation: Above the limit, degeneracy pressure fails and the star collapses into a neutron star or black hole.

questions.q: Chandrasekhar was related to which other Nobel laureate?
questions.explanation: Chandrasekhar was a nephew of C. V. Raman, who won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1930.

questions.q: The Chandrasekhar Limit applies to a star that has run out of what?
questions.explanation: A white dwarf is the remnant of a star that has exhausted its nuclear fuel and no longer fuses elements in its core.

questions.q: Chandrasekhar spent most of his academic career at which university?
questions.explanation: Chandrasekhar joined the University of Chicago in 1937 and remained there for the rest of his career.

questions.q: Chandrasekhar first worked out the mass limit during a voyage to England in which year?
questions.explanation: Chandrasekhar famously developed his ideas on the limit in 1930 while sailing from India to England to study at Cambridge.

questions.q: Which key physical effect did Chandrasekhar combine with degeneracy to derive the mass limit?
questions.explanation: He realized that at high densities electrons move relativistically, which softens degeneracy pressure and produces a finite maximum mass.

questions.q: Which famous astronomer publicly ridiculed Chandrasekhar's result at a 1935 Royal Astronomical Society meeting?
questions.explanation: Sir Arthur Eddington famously dismissed Chandrasekhar's collapse result, a clash that delayed acceptance for years.

questions.q: The exclusion principle underlying electron degeneracy pressure is named after which physicist?
questions.explanation: Electron degeneracy pressure arises from the Pauli exclusion principle, which forbids identical fermions from occupying the same quantum state.

questions.q: Which type of supernova is triggered when a white dwarf approaches the Chandrasekhar Limit by accreting matter?
questions.explanation: A Type Ia supernova results when a white dwarf nears the Chandrasekhar limit, igniting runaway carbon fusion.

questions.q: Because Type Ia supernovae explode at a near-fixed mass, they are valuable to astronomers as what?
questions.explanation: Their consistent peak brightness, set by the Chandrasekhar mass, makes Type Ia supernovae reliable standard candles for cosmic distances.

questions.q: With whom did Chandrasekhar share the 1983 Nobel Prize in Physics?
questions.explanation: He shared the 1983 prize with William A. Fowler, who was honored for work on nucleosynthesis in stars.

questions.q: At which English university did Chandrasekhar earn his PhD as a research student?
questions.explanation: Chandrasekhar completed his doctorate at the University of Cambridge, working at Trinity College.

questions.q: Roughly what is the size of a typical white dwarf, despite holding up to about a solar mass?
options: About the size of the Earth
About the size of the Sun
About the size of the Moon
questions.explanation: A white dwarf packs up to about 1.4 solar masses into a body roughly the size of the Earth.

questions.q: Which NASA space telescope, launched in 1999, is named in Chandrasekhar's honor?
questions.explanation: NASA's Chandra X-ray Observatory, launched in 1999, was named after Chandrasekhar, who was nicknamed Chandra.

questions.q: Chandrasekhar's mass limit shows that degeneracy pressure becomes less effective at supporting a star as which quantity increases?
questions.explanation: As mass and density rise, electrons turn relativistic and degeneracy pressure grows too slowly to resist gravity, defining the limit.

questions.q: Chandrasekhar served for nearly two decades as managing editor of which influential journal?
questions.explanation: Chandrasekhar was the long-serving managing editor of The Astrophysical Journal from 1952 to 1971.

questions.q: The Chandrasekhar mass depends inversely on the square of which composition-dependent quantity?
options: The mean molecular weight per electron
questions.explanation: The limit scales as the inverse square of the mean molecular weight per electron, so composition affects the exact value.

questions.q: In the relativistic limit, the equation of state of degenerate electrons behaves as a polytrope with which index?
options: A polytropic index n equal to 3
A polytropic index n equal to 1
A polytropic index n equal to 0
questions.explanation: Ultra-relativistic degenerate gas follows a polytrope of index n = 3, the case that yields a unique limiting mass.

questions.q: Chandrasekhar's limiting mass is mathematically expressed in terms of which fundamental constants?
options: Planck's constant, the speed of light, and the gravitational constant
The Boltzmann constant and Avogadro's number alone
questions.explanation: The limit combines the Planck constant, the speed of light, and the gravitational constant, with the proton mass, into a single mass scale.

questions.q: Around the same time as Chandrasekhar, which Soviet physicist independently derived a similar maximum mass for degenerate stars?
questions.explanation: Lev Landau independently arrived at comparable conclusions about a limiting mass for degenerate stellar matter in the early 1930s.

questions.q: Earlier work that introduced relativistic corrections to white dwarf structure, foreshadowing the limit, was done by whom?
questions.explanation: Edmund Stoner and Wilhelm Anderson had earlier estimated a limiting mass using relativistic degeneracy, which Chandrasekhar treated rigorously.

questions.q: For a typical carbon-oxygen white dwarf with mean molecular weight per electron near 2, the limit comes out to about what value?
questions.explanation: With a mean molecular weight per electron of about 2, the Chandrasekhar mass works out to roughly 1.4 solar masses.

questions.q: Beyond stellar structure, Chandrasekhar made landmark contributions to the theory of which random-motion process?
options: Stochastic and Brownian dynamics in astrophysics
questions.explanation: Chandrasekhar's celebrated 1943 review on stochastic problems brought Brownian-motion methods into dynamical astronomy.

questions.q: Late in his career, Chandrasekhar published a detailed mathematical study of which subject in general relativity?
options: The mathematical theory of black holes
The expansion rate of the universe
questions.explanation: His 1983 monograph, The Mathematical Theory of Black Holes, was a major treatise on black-hole spacetimes.
